issue_title: missing response header raises error rather than failure
issue_body:
The following reports an error when it should report a failure instead:

```yaml
tests:

  - name: failure
    url: http://google.com

    status: 302
    response_headers:
        x-foo: bar
```

AFAICT that's because internally a `KeyError` is raised ("'x-foo' header not available in response keys: dict_keys(...)"): `testtools.TestCase`'s default `exception_handlers` doesn't have a mapping for that particular exception, so it defaults to `_report_error` rather than using `_report_failure`.

issue_title: Regression with 1.39.0 and empty reponse_headers field
issue_body:
One of the tests in Gnocchi has an empty `reponse_headers` field:
https://github.com/gnocchixyz/gnocchi/blob/master/gnocchi/tests/functional/gabbits/resource.yaml#L503

This broke the test suite in some way as one behaviour changed between Gabbi 1.38.0 and 1.39.0. I've bisected and the culprit is 8de923ac825bfeb34cd8c9384634c68ebda345bd.

This is not critical as the fix is easy on Gnocchi's side: remove the empty and useless `reponse_headers` field in the YAML file https://github.com/gnocchixyz/gnocchi/pull/599. Still, I thought it might be useful to report it just in case there's something bigger behind that we would have missed.

issue_title: group keys in format documentation
issue_body:
The [Test Format](https://github.com/cdent/gabbi/blob/master/docs/source/format.rst) documentation page currently lists all keys in a lengthy list with seemingly arbitrary order.

This might be more comprehensible (and less daunting) if keys were grouped: metadata (name, description), request parameters, response expectations, settings (verbose, polling) - there might be more.

issue_title: Need a way to protect a region from cancellation (like "masking interrupts")
issue_body:
Here's an example of a simple bidirectional proxy using curio: https://gist.github.com/njsmith/4b4e75a9ab9e975705412f8f8e866948

The core idea is that we spawn two tasks to handle the copying in each direction:
```python
async def copy_all_bidir(sock1, sock2):
    await all_or_none([
        await curio.spawn(copy_all(sock1, sock2)),
        await curio.spawn(copy_all(sock2, sock1)),
])
```

The tricky part is managing these tasks. Basically with regards to exceptions/timeouts/cancellation/etc., we want it to act like the two `copy_all` tasks are both "part of" the `copy_all_bidir` task. So:
* If one of the tasks raises an exception, the other task should immediately be canceled, and the exception should propagate out of `copy_all_bidir`
* If both of the tasks complete, then `copy_all_bidir` should complete successfully
* If `copy_all_bidir` is cancelled, then both tasks should be canceled as part of `copy_all_bidir`'s cleanup

The `all_or_none` helper there tries to do this, but AFAICT it can't quite achieve the last requirement given curio's current APIs. The problem sequence is:

1. One of the tasks raises an exception
2. `all_or_none` catches this exception, and starts to cancel the other task
3. `all_or_none` itself gets canceled, thus canceling the cancellation and making everyone's head explode

My suggestion for what it should look like is:
```python
async def all_or_none(tasks):
    try:
        async for task in curio.wait(tasks):
            await task.join()
    except:
        async with curio.defer_cancellation:
            for task in tasks:
                await task.cancel()
        raise
```
except that `curio.defer_cancellation` isn't a thing that exists right now.

So this issue is to propose that `curio.defer_cancellation` be a thing that exists.

(Guess how I'm distracting myself from election news!)

issue_title: sep.extract() TypeError: can't concat bytes to str - Python 3.5.1
issue_body:
Hi, I've been running through a simple example of source detection with sep today using python 3.5.1 and sep 0.5.2. I came across a TypeError when trying to extract sources from an image. 

```python
>>> objects = sep.extract(data,thresh)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "sep.pyx", line 694, in sep.extract (sep.c:6883)
  File "sep.pyx", line 255, in sep._assert_ok (sep.c:3342)
TypeError: can't concat bytes to str
```

A colleague pointed out that this might be a python2 vs python3 issue. I tried running the same code in python2.7 and I got the following error:

```python
>>> objects = sep.extract(data,thresh)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "sep.pyx", line 694, in sep.extract (sep.c:6883)
  File "sep.pyx", line 255, in sep._assert_ok (sep.c:3357)
Exception: internal pixel buffer full: The limit of 300000 active object pixels over the detection threshold was reached. Check that the image is background subtracted and the detection threshold is not too low. If you need to increase the limit, use sep.set_extract_pixstack.
```

It turns out that I had not subtracted off the background and my threshold was too low, so it was flagging everything and running out of space in the pixel buffer. I've fixed my problem by subtracting the background from ```data``` before extraction, but I thought I should point out the issue with the error message being mangled on python3.

issue_title: add intrinsic rule(s) for testing processors kinds support
issue_body:
The modern standards provide many goodies about **kindness**, but *processors* (compilers) are free with respect how to implement them, e.g. Intel does not support `iso_ 10646` character kind whereas GNU does, thus we have

```fortran
print*, selected_char_kind('ascii')     ! => 1 for both GNU and Intel
print*, selected_char_kind('iso_10646') ! => 4 for GNU and => -1 for Intel
```

It could be very helpful if FoBiS provides an intrinsic rule(s) to test the compiler supported kindness, e.g.

```shell
FoBiS.py rule -support_char_kind 'iso_10646' # echo yes or not
FoBiS.py rule -support_real_kind '(33,4931)' # echo yes or not
```

Essentially, FoBiS will automatically create/compile/run/remove a tiny program for the introspective test like the following

```fortran
program fobis_rule_selected_char_kind
print*, selected_char_kind(#macro_passed_by_fobis)
endprogram fobis_rule_selected_char_kind

program fobis_rule_selected_int_kind
print*, selected_int_kind(#macro_passed_by_fobis)
endprogram fobis_rule_selected_int_kind

program fobis_rule_selected_real_kind
print*, selected_real_kind(#macro_passed_by_fobis)
endprogram fobis_rule_selected_real_kind
```
Upon execution, FoBiS *intercept* the output and if it is `>0` the compiler is assumed to support the queried kind.

import signal

# Ping callback function
def cb_ping(data):
    # will always print 11, because first element in data is firmata.SONAR = 0xb = 11
    #print(data[0])
    # will print 12 in this case; it's the pin-number of the attached sonar-device;
    # so here is the place to decide which device has called the callback (if you want to handle multiple devices with one callback)

    print(str(data[2]) + ' centimeters')

# Create a PyMata instance
board = PyMata("COM7", verbose=True)

def signal_handler(sig, frame):
    print('You pressed Ctrl+C!!!!')
    if board is not None:
        board.reset()
        board.close()
    sys.exit(0)

signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal_handler)

# Configure pin 12 for SONAR module (see png)
# every 100 ms it will trigger the sonar to meassure the distance and call the callback, if the value has changed compared to previous meassure
# AND the distance is <=100
 board.sonar_config(12, 12, cb_ping, 100, 100)
# if you just do:
#board.sonar_config(12 , 12, cb_ping)
# it will use 50ms as the ping interval and 200 centimeters (which is also the maximum)

# after 30 seconds stop; sleep will sleep the main-thread; so Ctrl-C will not work (at least under Windows) in this time
# so in this example you may also take out the signal_handler if you like; it will work, but directly 
# between the following sleep and the board.close
time.sleep(30)
board.close()

Hello,

If I try to connect using START_TLS to a server using its IP address instead of its hostname, it still works.

This is despite the fact that only the hostname is included in the certificate.

This allows man in the middle attacks because I might be trying to connect to server.example.org, but get a valid certificate from maninthemiddle.example.org instead, and I will send my credentials to the wrong server.

Ideally this needs to be configurable, as I do have some broken systems that do identify themselves with the wrong name.

This is with validate = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED

Looking at the code, it looks like it is suppose to check the certificate in the check_hostname function, so I am going to try and work out why that doesn't appear to be working (or if I am doing something wrong), and I will report back here.

Thanks

issue_body:
It seems that ``ldap3.SEQUENCE_TYPES`` is used to check for iterables. If this is correct I would prefer to check for iterables using ``isinstance(<iterable>, collections.Iterable)``. This would also cover ``set`` which are not sequences but also iterable. Otherwise I would like to change at least the code in the ``add`` method of ``Connection`` class which includes the following code::

    parm_object_class = list(object_class) if isinstance(object_class, SEQUENCE_TYPES) else [object_class]

I would change the is instancecheck to ``isinstance(object_class, SEQUENCE_TYPES + (set,))`` because I expect each object class appears only once which corresponds to a ``set``.

At the moment passing ``object_class`` of type ``set`` results in ``[set(...)]``.

issue_title: Best way to visualize in-place intermediate layer output from pre-trained dlib DNN?
issue_body:
First, thanks @davisking  for all of your work on and support of dlib.  I've learned an immense amount from creating a wrapper / bridge [addon for openFrameworks](https://github.com/bakercp/ofxDlib/) over the last year.  I love your [generic image interface](https://github.com/bakercp/ofxDlib/blob/develop/libs/ofxDlib/include/dlib/of_image.h)!

So, I don't think this is a dlib "issue", but a question about a best approach given the existing DNN architecture.

For pedagogical reasons (I'm teaching art students to use dlib), I would like to show my students the intermediate data layer outputs in a dlib DNN.  I'm going for something interactive and similar to this lovely [LeNet Vis](http://scs.ryerson.ca/~aharley/vis/conv/).

Currently it is easy to extract / view the layer parameters(convolution kernels, layer weights, etc), but trickier to view the outputs for each layer because outputs for many traditional layer types are processed in place.

Given a pretty thorough reading of the great docs and in-line comments [like these](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/dnn/core.h#L1544-L1558) I attempted to force non-in-place output by creating two versions of the LeNet like this:

```c++
using LeNet = dlib::loss_multiclass_log<
                                        dlib::fc<10,
                                        dlib::relu<dlib::fc<84,
                                        dlib::relu<dlib::fc<120,
                                        dlib::max_pool<2,2,2,2,dlib::relu<dlib::con<16,5,5,1,1,
                                        dlib::max_pool<2,2,2,2,dlib::relu<dlib::con<6,5,5,1,1,
                                        dlib::input<dlib::matrix<unsigned char>>
                                        >>>>>>>>>>>>;
 
template <typename SUBNET> using tlnet0  = dlib::add_tag_layer< 1100 + 0, SUBNET>;
// ... etc.

using TaggedLeNet = dlib::loss_multiclass_log<
                    tlnet0<dlib::fc<10,
                    tlnet1<dlib::relu<
                    tlnet2<dlib::fc<84,
                    tlnet3<dlib::relu<
                    tlnet4<dlib::fc<120,
                    tlnet5<dlib::max_pool<2,2,2,2,
                    tlnet6<dlib::relu<
                    tlnet7<dlib::con<16,5,5,1,1,
                    tlnet8<dlib::max_pool<2,2,2,2,
                    tlnet9<dlib::relu<
                    tlnet10<dlib::con<6,5,5,1,1,
                    dlib::input<dlib::matrix<unsigned char>
                    >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>;

```

I then trained the `LeNet` as described in the examples, serialize/deserialize and set the LeNet equal to my instance of `TaggedLeNet` like this:

```c++
LeNet _net;
dlib::deserialize("mnist_network.dat") >> _net;
TaggedLeNet net = _net;
```

Upon running my program, I get a runtime error:

```c++
auto& layer = dlib::layer<tlnet11,1>(net);
auto& layer_output = layer.get_output(); 
    // -> Runtime error: Accessing this layer's get_output() is disabled because an in-place layer has been stacked on top of it.
```

From my code read, this is happening because the state of `get_output_and_gradient_input_disabled` is getting serialized with the untagged net [here](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/dnn/core.h#L1279).  Then when I assign the deserialized `LetNet` to the `TaggedLeNet` (even though it's assignment compatible) it doesn't (for good reason I'm sure) recalculate the serialized values of `get_output_and_gradient_input_disabled` for each layer, even though the layers are surrounded by tag layers in the `TaggedLeNet`.  This all makes sense and is confirmed and is confirmed when I train `TaggedLeNet` directly, serialize/deserialize and receive no runtime errors

So, the question -- for small networks like `LeNet` it's easy to just retrain a tagged visualization version of the network.  But for some of the larger / more complex networks, I'd prefer not to retrain them.  I've considered some other ideas like creating partial networks that terminate in the outputs I'd like to visualize, but that starts to get pretty complicated and slow.

Does anyone have a suggestion about how best to visualize these intermediate layer outputs?

Thanks for reading this very long post.  CB

非常感谢 @houtianze  提供了这些方便的上传文件的方式， 非常感谢！
抱歉又来这里提 issue 啦， 因为是这个项目没有邮件列表， 好像没有别的可以提问题的地方.  

我用 bypy -e upload 上传整个目录的时候， 有一些疑问， 所以又来骚扰啦。 百度API 分片上传MD5可能会错误的问题就不说了，  很多其它的项目也都发现有这个问题。  我的问题是: 
1.  有的时候， 我用 bypy -e compare 命令比较， 发现有几个文件md5 不一致，  但是有时候会提示直接调用了 combine 接口，不用重新上传文件， 直接就重新生成md5， 往往这个时候远程服务器上的md5值就正确了，     但是为什么有些其它文件就没有调用combine 接口，    为什么都是在一个目录， 同时刚刚上传过的文件会有这种差异呢，  我理解如果每个差异文件都能走进这个流程， 可能就不用文件重新上传啦。

issue_title: dbus warning produced on macOS
issue_body:
The following is what I see when I import keyring in Python 3 on a macOS 10.13 system:
```
# python3                                                                                                 library/repos/turf
Python 3.5.5 (default, Mar 29 2018, 16:22:58)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 9.0.0 (clang-900.0.39.2)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import keyring
dbus[2365]: Dynamic session lookup supported but failed: launchd did not provide a socket path, verify that org.freedesktop.dbus-session.plist is loaded!
dbus[2365]: Dynamic session lookup supported but failed: launchd did not provide a socket path, verify that org.freedesktop.dbus-session.plist is loaded!
```
My copy of the keyring package was installed using pip:
```
# pip3 list | grep -i keyring
keyring                            13.0.0
The documentation for keyring mentions the need for dbus on Linux, but does not appear to say anything about a similar requirement for macOS, yet simply importing keyring results in the message about "dbus" shown above.
```
From the symptoms, it seems as if keyring unconditionally tries to load the dbus module even on macOS. From a quick look at the code, I could not immediately see _where_ that was happening, but if someone could point me in the right direction I could see about creating a PR to make it stop.

issue_title: What is the behavior if connection/login status drops?
issue_body:
The API documentation says to use login() to log in, then sync() periodically to make changes.

What happens if, for instance, the connection is dropped in between the login() and sync() calls? Will sync() automatically re-login, or does it silently fail?

If the latter, is there some way to check login() status to know if we need to login again?

The use case for this is, I'm trying to keep a Keep note synced with a KDE Plasma note. I have a script running that periodically checks, every 5 minutes or so, if the note has changed to sync the two. So my question is, should I just send a new login() request every 5 minutes? Or should I just login() once, then every five minutes call sync()? Or login() once, then every five minutes check login status and possibly re-login again, and then call sync()? etc.

I can't find anything on this in the documentation, so it might be good to add something there.

issue_title: Passing unicode into minidom
issue_body:
This issue concerns the latest 'requests' based osmapi.

I think the use of requests 'response.text' in '_http_request' and thus passing unicode into minidom is problematic.  I think the proper way to do this is to use the raw data 'request.content' for passing into minidom.  Subsequently minidom will read the encoding from the xml header (e.g. '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>') and handle the raw data occording to this encoding information.

I seem to be able to trigger unicode issues with the following code:

import osmapi
a = osmapi.OsmApi()
c = a.ChangesetDownload(37393499)

which generates this unicode exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "osmapi/OsmApi.py", line 1324, in ChangesetDownload
    return self.ParseOsc(data)
  File "osmapi/OsmApi.py", line 1751, in ParseOsc
    data = xml.dom.minidom.parseString(data)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/xml/dom/minidom.py", line 1928, in parseString
    return expatbuilder.parseString(string)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/xml/dom/expatbuilder.py", line 940, in parseString
    return builder.parseString(string)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/xml/dom/expatbuilder.py", line 223, in parseString
    parser.Parse(string, True)
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xf8' in position 11709: ordinal not in range(128)

Changing '_http_request' to use 'request.content' corrects this issue.

issue_title: Enveloped signature: Signature-placeholder not found when not direct child of root
issue_body:
Hello,

According to then W3C specification an enveloped signature is
> **Signature, Enveloped**
> The signature is over the XML content that contains the signature as an element. The content provides the root XML document element. Obviously, enveloped signatures must take care not to include their own value in the calculation of the SignatureValue.

There is no need for the signature element to be a direct child of the root element.

So you may use

    <TheInformation>
        <SomeInfo>
            ...
            <Security>
                ...
                <Signature>  
                    ...
                </Signature>  
                ...
            </Security>
            ...
        <\SomeInfo>
        <SomeOtherInfo>
            ...
        <\SomeOtherInfo>
        ...
    </TheInformation>

And there are many implementations which put the _signature_ element **somewhere** in the
XML-tree.

Your implementation (method _\_getPayload\_c14n_) uses

    .
    .
    signature_placeholders = self._findall(self.data, "Signature[@Id='placeholder']")
    .
    .

to find the position of the signature element and _\_findall_ is defined as

    def _findall(self, element, query, namespace="ds"):
        return element.findall(namespace + ":" + query, namespaces=namespaces)

But the element-method _findall_ only searches for direct childs if called with a simple name

    element.findall('ds:Signature')

To find all descendants you have to search with an x-path  expression beginning with './/' 

    element.findall('.//ds:Signature')

I changed the definition of _\_findall_ to

    def _findall(self, element, query, namespace="ds"):
        return element.findall('.//' + namespace + ":" + query, namespaces=namespaces)

and now the Program behaves correctly. 

But since you use your _\_findall_ method in several places, I don't know if I destroyed the other uses of this routine. 
And until now I didn't find the time to analyze, if the non-recursive search occurs in other places of your program (I think your _verify_ function does also not work for the above example).

regards  Volker

issue_title: Wrong completion for arbitrary argument lists
issue_body:
Accroding to [PEP-484](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/#arbitrary-argument-lists-and-default-argument-values), for the following function declaration:
```python
def foo(*args: str, **kwds: int):
```
> In the body of function foo, the type of variable args is deduced as Tuple[str, ...] and the type of variable kwds is Dict[str, int].

I am afraid jedi suggests completions for `args` as if its type were `str`:
![args](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2445711/32145388-f84eb90a-bcc7-11e7-8a4e-caa8c076c566.png)
 and for `kwds` as if its type were `int`:
![kwds](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2445711/32145390-f8766a40-bcc7-11e7-96e9-9f538136fc49.png)

The examples are from my `IPython 6.2.1` running `Python 3.6.3` and `jedi==0.11.0`. I observed this behavior when I was using the `deoplete-jedi` plugin in NeoVim, but I just wanted to provide a simpler reproduction.

issue_title: What's a good method of making a Python package depend on nodeenv and node.js packages?
issue_body:
Currently, I have a few (unpublished) Python packages in local use, which I install (for development purposes) with a Bash script on Linux into an activated (otherwise "empty") virtual environment in the following manner:

```bash
cd /root/of/python/package
pip install -r requirements_python.txt # includes "nodeenv"
nodeenv -p # pulls node.js and integrates it into my virtual environment
npm i -g npm # update npm ...
cat requirements_node.txt | xargs npm install -g
pip install -e .
```

The background is that I have a number of node.js dependencies, JavaScript CLI scripts, which are called by my Python code.

Pros of current approach:

* dead simple: relies on `nodeenv` for all required plumbing
* can theoretically be implemented within setup.py with `subprocess.Popen` etc

Cons of current approach:

* Unix-like platforms with Bash only
* "hard" to distribute my packages, say on PyPI
* requires a virtual environment created by the user beforehand (?)
* has potentially "interesting" side effects if a package is installed globally
* potentially interferes with a pre-existing configuration / "deployment" of `nodeenv` in the current virtual environment (?)

I have been researching and thinking about this issue for a while. My original question was whether there was a canonical or just any sane, potentially cross-platform approach of defining `node.js` dependencies for a Python package, making it publishable. I learned that there is no canonical way, though there is for instance a (still relatively young) project for defining `node.js` dependencies in a Python package in a well thought-through manner: [`calmjs`](https://github.com/calmjs/calmjs). After getting in touch with its developer, I learned that `calmjs` explicitly does not ensure that there is `node.js` or `npm` configured, up and running. This part is still left to the user or developer of a package relying on it. Realizing that defining a number of `node.js` dependencies is just half the story (or even less), I'd like to post this question / issue here, seeking advise on how to potentially make a Python package depend on `nodeenv`. I have this vague idea of something like a set of instructions in a `setup.py` taking care of something along the lines of `nodeenv -p` with as little as possible collateral damage ... 

**What's a good method of making a Python package depend on nodeenv (and Node.js packages)?**

*For reference, I posted a [related question on stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/q/49953785/1672565) asking more or less the same thing - there is currently an open bounty on it. A related discussion in `calmjs`' issue tracker can be found [here](https://github.com/calmjs/calmjs/issues/40).*

issue_title: psutil fails to return the nice value for some processes on Solaris 10
issue_body:
How to reproduce - chose xntp on Solaris 10:

    >>> p = psutil.Process(960)
    >>> p.name()
    'xntpd'
    >>> p.nice()
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/__init__.py", line 755, in nice
	return self._proc.nice_get()
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 346, in wrapper
	return fun(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 451, in nice_get
	return cext_posix.getpriority(self.pid)
    OSError: [Errno 22] Invalid argument

The system's ps is able to return the nice value:

    $ ps -e -o pid,uid,gid,ruid,nice | grep 960
       960     0     0     0 RT

A simple C test program that just calls `getpriority()` on that PID also fails with EINVAL.

Perhaps `getpriority()` always fails for realtime processes, on Solaris 10. Is kind of plausible - since priorities doesn't really make sense for realtime. The man page doesn't include details on that topic, though.

This breaks `psutil.process_iter()` unless one uses a non-default attribute set that doesn't include `'nice'`. Example how to make it fail with a minimal attribute set:

    >>> for p in psutil.process_iter(): print(p.as_dict(attrs=['nice']))
    {'nice': 0}
    {'nice': -20}
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/__init__.py", line 574, in as_dict
	ret = meth()
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/__init__.py", line 755, in nice
	return self._proc.nice_get()
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 346, in wrapper
	return fun(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 451, in nice_get
	return cext_posix.getpriority(self.pid)
    OSError: [Errno 22] Invalid argument

The psutil Solaris-specific code [contains a note][1] regarding `getpriority()` vs. `psinfo` that mentions issue #1082.

There are several ways how to fix this:

- call `getpriority()` first and fall back to psinfo
- switch to psinfo as it's good enough for `ps`
- default to 0 if `getpriority()` fails

See also:

issue_title: Client never chooses IPv6 after bootstrapping
issue_body:
If you provide IPv6 addresses when creating the client (e.g., `bootstrap_servers=["[::1]:9092"]`) and the server responds with a hostname (e.g., `localhost`), this library always actually uses IPv4. This is because the `get_ip_port_afi` function, when passed a hostname, always returns and address family of `AF_INET`.

Ideally, I think this code should be restructured to use `getaddrinfo` and a loop to detect whether the host is available over v4 or v6. It looks like there's no actual resolution in `kafka/conn.py`; we're relying on implicit resolution done by passing a hostname to `socket.connect_ex`; hopefully that uses getaddrinfo behind the scenes and follows RFC 3484.

issue_title: Problems committing offsets during the rebalance
issue_body: I have a consumer reading a single partition. The code is: ``` for message in self.consumer: result = make_love(message) # up to 10 seconds; it's a long time, believe me! make_baby(result) try: self.consumer.commit() except IllegalGenerationError: logging.error('Illegal generation caught! Skipping it, huh') ``` As far as I know, it goes as follows: 1) Consumer picks the next message from the queue. 2) Heavy processing routine is executed. 3) GroupCoordintor performs rebalancing, thus incrementing the current generation number. 4) A new object is created using `result` as an argument. 5) Consumer commits the offset of the successfully processed message. 6) GroupCoordinator responses with `IllegalGenerationError` because while consumer has been processing the task, actual generation number has incremented so that the consumer becomes illegal. 7) Consumer reconnects, fetches the very message it has processed already (but failed to commit), resulting in spawning twin objects (undesired behaviour). Meanwhile, [the docs] (https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/Kafka+0.9+Consumer+Rewrite+Design#Kafka0.9ConsumerRewriteDesign-Consumer) say the following: > The consumer connects to the co-ordinator and sends a HeartbeatRequest. If an IllegalGeneration error code is returned in the HeartbeatResponse, it indicates that the co-ordinator has initiated a rebalance. The consumer then stops fetching data, commits offsets and sends a JoinGroupRequest to it's co-ordinator broker. In the JoinGroupResponse, it receives the list of topic partitions that it should own and the new generation id for it's group. At this time, group management is done and the consumer starts fetching data and (optionally) committing offsets for the list of partitions it owns. Of course, I can `commit()` at first and `make_baby()` thereafter, but in case of `make_baby()` failure it would be at-most-once processing, so it's an undesired behavior, too. The question is: should the consumer be able to successfully commit its offsets if it gets an `IllegalGenerationError`? If not, how can I perform the exactly-once semantics?

Here's a slightly modified version of the client example:

```python
import asyncio
from aio_pika.robust_connection import RobustConnection

import aio_pika


def on_amqp_lost(connection):
    print('###### on_ampq_lost', connection)


async def ampq(current_loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop):
    connection = await aio_pika.connect_robust(
        "amqp://amqp_user:**********@ip-*********.eu-west-1.compute.internal:5672",
        reconnect_interval=10,
        loop=current_loop
    )  # type: RobustConnection

    connection.add_connection_lost_callback(on_amqp_lost)

    async with connection:

        channel = await connection.channel()

        queue = await channel.declare_queue("some_queue", durable=True)

        print('###### connected')

        async for message in queue:
            with message.process():
                print(message.body)


def on_loop_exception(current_loop, context):
    exc = context.get('exception')
    print('###### on_loop_exception %r ' % (exc,))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    asyncio.ensure_future(ampq(loop))
    loop.set_exception_handler(on_loop_exception)
    loop.run_forever()
```

This connects fine:

```
###### connected
b'{"message": "hello"}'
```

After restarting the RabbitMQ server (takes about 3 seconds) an _Auto Reconnect Error_ is thrown but the connection seems to recover

```
###### on_ampq_lost amqp://amqp_user:********@ip-*********.eu-west-1.compute.internal:5672
###### on_loop_exception ConnectionError('Auto Reconnect Error',) 
b'{"message": "hello"}'

```

If I shut down the RabbitMQ server I get the last 3 exceptions shown below repeatedly:

```
###### on_ampq_lost amqp://amqp_user:********@ip-*********.eu-west-1.compute.internal:5672
###### on_loop_exception ConnectionError('Auto Reconnect Error',) 
###### on_loop_exception AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'channel'",) 
###### on_loop_exception ConnectionError('Auto Reconnect Error',) 
```

Once the RabbitMQ server is up again the connection recovers.

How can I avoid these exceptions? In my use case there are other connections open on the same loop and I want to stop it once any of these disconnects.

issue_title: Add support for Expect-CT
Issues with moving to OpenSSL 1.1.0
issue_body: In OpenSSL 1.1.0, support for SSL 2.0 and export cipher suites was removed. This is good for the Internet but bad for SSLyze. We also cannot stay stuck on 1.0.2 forever as new cipher suites (ChaCha20 and Poly1305) were added 1.1.0. This might require writing a plugin to check for cipher suites and protocols that are not supported by OpenSSL.

issue_title: ROBOT false positive
issue_body:
guide.duo.com is a domain hosted on google app engine, which is probably run by boringssl in the background.

The robot check by sslyze reports this as vulnerable, but it's almost certainly a false positive. The original robot-detect script can't test this host, as it requires SNI and we didn't implement SNI.

The same host has been falsely detected by ssllabs and they fixed it in their dev version:
#549

/CC @kelbyludwig

    import parsedatetime as pdt

        ptc = pdt.Constants()

        cal = pdt.Calendar(ptc)
    
        result, parsed_as = cal.parse(human_readable)

issue_title: wordcloud.wordcloud.fit_words() has wrong(not-updated?) doc string
issue_body:
def fit_words(self, frequencies):
        """Create a word_cloud from words and frequencies.
        Alias to generate_from_frequencies.
        Parameters
        ----------
        frequencies : array of tuples
            A tuple contains the word and its frequency.
        Returns
        -------
        self
        """
        return self.generate_from_frequencies(frequencies)

In fact, the frequencies paramenter should be a dict from string to float, see below:

    def generate_from_frequencies(self, frequencies, max_font_size=None):
        """Create a word_cloud from words and frequencies.
        Parameters
        ----------
        frequencies : dict from string to float
            A contains words and associated frequency.

issue_title: Does `_locateAll_opencv` func require exactly same resolution to match?
issue_body:
I just took an experiment.
Have a picture as the haystack, cut a part from it as the needle, put them into `_locateAll_opencv` func and get the expected return.
Then scale the haystack image a little bit (like 90%), while leave the needle untouched, put the original needle and new (90% scaled) haystack into `_locateAll_opencv` func, but get nothing.
The `confidence` param wouldn't help, it either returns nothing (when `confidence` is still high enough), or just throws out a bunch of false tuples without the expected match (when `confidence` is very low, which make sense in the case), but just can not find that correct but scaled part of needle.

So, is resolution the primary condition to meet? Even `confidence` won't make any differences here?

issue_title: Scaling issue on Windows affecting screenshots
issue_body:
Copying this email that was sent to me:

I am trying to take a screenshot using the pyautogui.screenshot() function. It takes the screenshot and saves it properly. But the problem is that the picture is way too small. It does not capture the whole screen. And if I take screenshot with some region like region=(0, 0, 150, 690) it captures only about (0, 0, 120, 547). I am not a great programmer, but I think that the problem is not in pyautogui but in PIL. Because PIL.ImageGrab.grab() gives also too small pictures!

I am using Python 3.5.2 (v3.5.2:4def2a2901a5) and it is 64 bit. My pyautogui version that I am using is '0.9.33' and my PIL version is '3.4.2'.
And windows 10.

I did more research and found this article on stackoverflow which worked for me. I had to change the "Disable display scaling on high DPI settings" to true on every python.exe file.
Link: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24370228/python-imaging-library-fails-to-grab-whole-screen?answertab=active#tab-top

issue_title: tld.get_tld_names() documented as returning iterable, but Trie object is not iterable
issue_body:
I use this package in a number of places for both personal and work projects; firstly I would just like to say thank you for sharing your hard work!

`get_tld_names()` in previous versions returned an iterable, and is still documented as doing so. I'm not exactly sure which version it was introduced in, however, in the latest version (v0.9 at time of writing), this function returns a Trie object.

Looking through the source code, it appears that the Trie class is not implemented in a way that makes it iterable (previous implementation used a built-in `set()` data structure). If I have additional time I will try to provide a pull request, however, work has been leaving very little free-time lately.

Thank you again!

issue_title: Reading a file object can't guess the format, but won't allow it as an argument either.
issue_body:
Consider this code:

    from pysoundfile import read, write
    from tempfile import NamedTemporaryFile

    tmp_file = NamedTemporaryFile(suffix='.wav')
    write(signal, tmp_file, fs, format='wav')

So far, this works fine, but you can't read the file:

    read(tmp_file) # raises RuntimeError('File contains data in an unknown format.')
    read(tmp_file, format='wav') # raises TypeError("Not allowed for existing files (except 'RAW'): [...] format [...]")

In this particular case, `read(tmp_file.name)` works fine, but not every `file` object has a name.

issue_title: time_to_tick generates incorrect timing due to casting from float to int.
issue_body:
time_to_tick does return int(tick) at the end. But it should be round(int(tick)). 

I attached an example midi file. It's a metronome with a tempo map. 
[example.zip](https://github.com/craffel/pretty-midi/files/242900/example.zip)
If you mididump.py the file you'll see that all NoteOn events have tick=360 and all NoteOffs have tick=120

If you read it and write it:
pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI('/tmp/example_accel.mid').write('/tmp/testing.mid')
you'll see that one of the NoteOn events has a tick=359 and one of the NoteOffs has a tick=120.

If you change time_to_tick to be round(int(tick)) the timing is correct. 

I haven't thought this through. It could be that rounding will introduce unintended problems elsewhere.

issue_title: Relation "migratehistory" already exists
issue_body:
When I try to run all unapplied migrations, I get the following error:
```
peewee.ProgrammingError: relation "migratehistory" already exists
```
My python script to run all migrations:

```
db_connection.init_database()
db_connection.connect()
database = db_connection.get_database()

router = Router(database)

# Run all unapplied migrations
router.run()
```
After quick investigation to the source of peewee_migrate I found this from `BaseRouter`:
```
    @cached_property
    def model(self):
        """Initialize and cache MigrationHistory model."""
        MigrateHistory._meta.database = self.database
        MigrateHistory._meta.db_table = self.migrate_table
        MigrateHistory.create_table(True)
        return MigrateHistory
```
Looks like it tries to create the table always because of the last line before return which then fails if I have already migrated something to the db and the table exists. Why is that? Am I using this wrong somehow?

issue_title: Why is `BaseResource._ids` yielding primary keys twice ?
issue_body:
The `BaseResource._ids` property yields all so called _primary keys_ twice, once as the value of the attribute itself, once as the value of the attribute cast to a string with `str`.

Several questions about that:
- Why doing so ? What is the use case ?
- If there is a use case indeed, nothing prevents me to use an array, dict or object attribute as a key. What would be the meaning of `str(object)` ?

Not convinced that works well and since it is not used anywhere in the code, not sure there actually is a need for this.

Also as a side note, I think the name [`_pks`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unique_key) is very much misleading. An entity can only have several keys but only one _*p*rimary *k*ey_ (which is where I guess _pk_ in `_pks` is coming from).

I welcome any feedback on this. Thanks in advance.

issue_title: python2-speedtest-cli depends upon python-setuptools  posted at bugs.CentOS.org.
issue_body:
I can see this github project does not supply RPM packages.  But for those looking to solve this....

CentOS6 rpm package needs to declare a dependency on python-setuptools. 
[ Bug report "**python2-speedtest-cli depends upon python-setuptools**" ](https://bugs.centos.org/view.php?id=140420)

```
$ speedtest
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/speedtest", line 5, in <module>
    from pkg_resources import load_entry_point
ImportError: No module named pkg_resources
```

This may apply to CentOS7 as well, but have not tested from a relatively pristine environment.  

To fix:
On CentOS6: `yum install python-setuptools`
On Ubuntu: `apt-get install python-setuptools`
[StackOverflow.com answer **No module named pkg_resources**](https://stackoverflow.com/a/31200131/711422)

issue_title: "assume" in an always failing test gives hypothesis.errors.UnsatisfiedAssumption
issue_body:
So, I've been tinkering around with Hypothesis the last few days, and I've run into a fun one. In a couple of cases I've had a test that should always throw an exception because I've broken something in the code it's testing *and* has an `assume` statement at the beginning of the test function, I get a hypothesis.errors.UnsatisfiedAssumption not the expected Exception. Fixing the broken code makes the test pass, but I'd much rather have the exception to make my life easier when fixing the code...

I suspect a chunk of this is that I'm doing something a bit daft - testing a heavily stateful interface without use of `RuleBasedStateMachine` yet - and the `assume` is of the form "don't do a duplicate insert of an item with one of these ids that needs to be unique", so it may be failing due to trying to simplify something it can't run twice, but I'd still expect this to throw the Exception.

I'm using Hypothesis 1.18.1 from pypi on Python 3.5.0 on OS X

issue_title: urllib3 response object doesn't expose an iterator
issue_body:
So this is a curious one - the urllib3 response object inherits io.Base which does provide an iterator over itself, however this iterator doesn't invoke the response object stream() method which handles negotiated chunked responses.

This can be seen explicitly when strace'ing a process using the SSEClient, there will be tons of 1 byte recvfrom syscalls which certainly should not happen when parsing chunked encoding since the chunk-size should be the reads (until the next chunk etc). 

The fix is pretty simple, change `for chunk in self._event_source:` to `for chunk in self._event_source.stream():` Although I'm uncertain if this is portable to other response objects (although `requests` just uses urllib3 under the hood anyways).

If I get some time I present a more detailed example... speaking of which is there a good public event-stream API that would serve for examples? That would be convenient.

issue_title: Error on loading of sensors JSON after inpropper shutdown
issue_body:
### Issue description:
Stack trace from startup of Home-Assistant 0.16

```
ERROR:homeassistant.bootstrap:Error during setup of component mysensors
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/homeassistant/bootstrap.py", line 105, in _setup_component
    if not component.setup(hass, config):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/homeassistant/components/mysensors.py", line 111, in setup
    port, persistence, persistence_file, version, baud_rate)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/homeassistant/components/mysensors.py", line 75, in setup_gateway
    baud=baud_rate)
  File "/home/pi/.homeassistant/lib/mysensors/mysensors.py", line 280, in __init__
    persistence_file, protocol_version)
  File "/home/pi/.homeassistant/lib/mysensors/mysensors.py", line 32, in __init__
    self._load_sensors()
  File "/home/pi/.homeassistant/lib/mysensors/mysensors.py", line 165, in _load_sensors
    self._perform_file_action(self.persistence_file, 'load')
  File "/home/pi/.homeassistant/lib/mysensors/mysensors.py", line 180, in _perform_file_action
    func(filename)
  File "/home/pi/.homeassistant/lib/mysensors/mysensors.py", line 148, in _load_json
    self.sensors = json.load(file_handle, cls=MySensorsJSONDecoder)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/json/__init__.py", line 268, in load
    parse_constant=parse_constant, object_pairs_hook=object_pairs_hook, **kw)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/json/__init__.py", line 331, in loads
    return cls(**kw).decode(s)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/json/decoder.py", line 343, in decode
    obj, end = self.raw_decode(s, idx=_w(s, 0).end())
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/json/decoder.py", line 361, in raw_decode
    raise ValueError(errmsg("Expecting value", s, err.value)) from None
ValueError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

This happened after an improper shutdown of Home Assistant / pymysensors. (A terminal window running 'hass' was closed.) 

The issue is probably that the sensors JSON file is updated / over written on each sensor update, and there are no fall-back if the file is not completed before a shutdown / or power loss.

Reproduce by replacing the sensor JSON file with an empty file.

### Workaround
Remove the JSON file from the filesystem, and let mysensors populate it again.

### Possible fix
When writing the JSON file. Do this in a tree step process. 
1. Move the current JSON file to a temporary filename. (mysensors.json => mysensors.json_old)
2. Write the sensor JSON file to disk, as previous.
3. Remove the temporary JSON file.

When reading the JSON file. Do this process.
1. Read sensor JSON file if present.
2. If this fails Copy the temporary JSON file to the sensor JSON file name.
3. Read sensor JSON file if present.
4. If this fails revert to an empty JSON file, and set an error message in the log.
5. Always delete the temporary JSON file if present.

issue_title: Can voluptuous play well with pyparsing?
issue_body:
I am wondering how good it could be to have a native support for `pyparsing` in `voluptuous`. They are both some really powerful packages and I have the intuition they could play well together. 

Isn't that schema nice?

```
s = Schema( Suppress(':') + Word('abc') + Suppress(':') )
s(':aabbcc:')
aabbcc   
```

A first step could be easily achieved by making a pyparsing `ParserElement` callable and then retrieve its first element: 

```
class PyparsingInvalid(Invalid):
    pass

class Pyparsing(object):
    def __call__(self, value):
        try:
            return self.pyparsing_schema.parseString(value)[0]
        except ParseException as e:
            raise PyparsingInvalid(e.msg)
    def __init__(self, schema):
        self.pyparsing_schema = stringStart + schema + stringEnd
```

The minor difference with the initial idea is the need to explicitly cast a `ParseElement` to a `Schema`:
 
```
s = Schema( Pyparsing(Suppress(':') + Word('abc') + Suppress(':')) )
```

In order to enhance the synergy between `pyparsing` and `voluptuous` it would be good to make both packages *more* compatible. This could be done on both sides: 

1. pyparsing `ParseElement`

Making `__call__` not set the default result name, but instead call `parseString` which will result in an unwanted compatibility break. 

2. voluptuous

Detect if the input is a `pyparsing.ParserElement` and automatically wrap the `Pyparsing` class around it. 

```
try: 
    from pyparsing import ParserElement as _parserElement
except ImportError: 
    class _parserElement(): 
       pass
...

def wrap_pyparsing(element):
    if isinstance(element, _parseElement):
        return Pyparsing(element)
    return element
```

One last point to consider is to define what should `Pyparsing` return once called: 

1. An instance of `ParseResults` which should not be very useful in a voluptuous schema
2. The first element: `foo.parseString(bar)[0]`
3. A `list` or a `dict` with `list(foo.parseString(bar)[0])`
4. Something configurable by the user using either a `kwargs` or a global setting. 

Regarding this latter point, the most straightforward way to increase the synergy would be to keep the proposed `Pyparsing` and add to it the ability to choose what output the user wants: 

```
Schema(Pyparsing(Suppress(':') + Word('abc') + Suppress(':'), output=lambda x: x[0]))
```

Any better idea?

issue_title: nested tag issue, beyond scope of library?
issue_body:
I'm having a hard time understanding why the output I am getting is formatted the way it is.

## Admonition

I think this may be "beyond" the scope of capabilities of this library.  I don't know much about the formal `xml` standards, but I suspect that the tags parsed correctly such as `para` or `orderedlist` are standardized where as the breaking ones (e.g. `computeroutput`) are not?

I don't think the application purpose is relevant, but I'm playing around with the doxygen xml output.  These tags are defined in a `compound.xsd` document I can include if desired, but some correctly parsed (`orderedlist`) tags are defined in this -- AKA I'm not sure if this is a bug or unsupported feature.

## Breaking input

```py
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import textwrap
import xmltodict
import json

raw_xml = textwrap.dedent('''
<detaileddescription>
    <para>
        There <computeroutput>once</computeroutput> was a lad who wanted to accomplish things.
    </para>
    <para>
        <orderedlist>
            <listitem><para>He did stuff. There was nothing else he could do.</para></listitem>
            <listitem><para>He enumerated stuff.</para></listitem>
            <listitem><para>He listed stuff. </para></listitem>
        </orderedlist>
    </para>
</detaileddescription>
''')

xml_dict = xmltodict.parse(raw_xml)
print(json.dumps(xml_dict, indent=4))

print("*" * 77)

print(xmltodict.unparse(xml_dict, pretty=True))
```

### Output json

```json
{
    "detaileddescription": {
        "para": [
            {
                "computeroutput": "once",
                "#text": "There  was a lad who wanted to accomplish things."
            },
            {
                "orderedlist": {
                    "listitem": [
                        {
                            "para": "He did stuff. There was nothing else he could do."
                        },
                        {
                            "para": "He enumerated stuff."
                        },
                        {
                            "para": "He listed stuff."
                        }
                    ]
                }
            }
        ]
    }
}
```

### Output xml

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<detaileddescription>
	<para>
		<computeroutput>once</computeroutput>
There  was a lad who wanted to accomplish things.	</para>
	<para>
		<orderedlist>
			<listitem>
				<para>He did stuff. There was nothing else he could do.</para>
			</listitem>
			<listitem>
				<para>He enumerated stuff.</para>
			</listitem>
			<listitem>
				<para>He listed stuff.</para>
			</listitem>
		</orderedlist>
	</para>
</detaileddescription>
```

What leads the `computeroutput` tag to behave this way?  If you think it's easy to patch I'm down to take a look with some guidance, I **love** being able to parse xml dictionary style :smile:

issue_title: Librabry does not support Kakao
Implement Constrained Objective Functions
issue_body:
## Context

For the v.0.2.0 release (#5), we're planning to add two new major features, and one of them is constrained optimization via PSO (#8). As with PySwarms, we don't only provide primitives for optimizers, but also built-in objective functions to test various methods (such as in the [single-objective case](https://pyswarms.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/pyswarms.utils.functions.html#module-pyswarms.utils.functions.single_obj)). In this Issue, we will be implementing constrained objective functions.

## What you'll do

- [ ] Write a module `pyswarms.utils.functions.contrained_obj` containing **two** constrained objective functions (let's do two for now).
- [ ] Create unit tests for the said functions in the module `tests.utils.functions.test_constrainedobj`.

### What makes this different from the `single_obj` module?

Single objective functions takes an input vector, and computes the *fitness* of that vector given an equation. So if your objective function is the [sphere function](https://pyswarms.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/pyswarms.utils.functions.html#pyswarms.utils.functions.single_obj.sphere_func), you simply supply it by an input vector, say `[0,0]`, and it gives you its corresponding fitness i.e., `0`.

Constrained objective functions are different in a sense that you don't only have a method that computes a vector's fitness, but also a constraint where these vectors are limited. These are useful in applications where you want to optimize with respect to a certain limit, say, in mechanical engineering where the yield stress should not go overboard a value `x`. Of course, you can have multiple constraints in a given problem, and we will consider that here.

### What should the data structure look like?

Unlike single-objective functions where we only have one method per function, constrained optimizations will take on a more object-oriented approach. *Before*, we have something like this:

```python
def objective_function(X):
    # Compute a fitness given X, say, just (X-5)**2
    J = (X-5)**2
    return J
```

*Now,* we will have something like:

```python
class MyConstrainedObjective(object):
    """My constrained objective function

    Most constrained optimization problems are defined in the following manner:
    min J(X) = something something, such that
        equality constraint: h(x), g(x)
        inequality constraint: i(x), j(x)
    """

    @classmethod
    def objective_func(cls, X):
        """Contains the actual fitness function that computes for an input X"""
        # Compute for J
        return J

    @classmethod
    def ineq_cons(cls):
        """Returns all inequality constraints of the given problem"""
        # Assuming we have two inequality constraints
        return (cls.ineq_1, cls.ineq_2)

    @classmethod
    def eq_cons(cls):
       """Returns all equality constraints of the given problem"""
      # Assuming we only have one equality constraint
      return cls.eq_1

    @staticmethod
    def ineq_1(X):
       """Inequality constraint 1 that checks X"""
       return X <= 5 # for example

    # the same goes for ineq_2 and eq_1
```

If you think there's a better data model to such, feel free to suggest a better way of handling these constraints. This means that when we're performing a constrained optimization problem, we only need to call the objective function, and the inequality and equality constraints if possible:

```python
cons_pso = ConstrainedPSO()
cons_pso.optimize(objective_func=MyConstrainedObjective.objective_func, 
                  ineq_cons=MyConstrainedObjective.ineq_cons
                  eq_cons=MyConstrainedObjective.eq_cons)
```
A good resource for good constrained optimization problems can be in this [link](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Test_functions_for_optimization#Test_functions_for_constrained_optimization). It's Wikipedia I know, but this one is pretty accurate. For starters, you can try the first two methods.

### Writing tests

You can check on how we write tests in the `tests` module  of this package. Currently, we're using the `unittest` package to do these tests. Some of the things we'd like to check are the following:

- Does the objective function approximately return the minima when fed with argmin (the argument that gives the optimal value)?
- Do the methods raise an Error when fed with values outside of bounds, or of wrong type etc?
- Other things you can think of that increases test coverage. Meaning, all methods you write should be tested.

### Other things you need to know

- PySwarms is pretty much a documentation junkie so please put docstrings in all classes, methods, and modules you'll create. This is important so that others can easily pick-up what you're working on. You can refer to the [numpy documentation](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/HOWTO_DOCUMENT.rst.txt) for a primer on how we do things.
- We also follow [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#introduction) rules for styling Python code. If you wish to check your code against PEP8, please run a `flake8` test on your file.
- This whole Issue may be quite long and overwhelming at first, but don't worry! I also wanted to help you make your first contribution! And hope we both learn in the process. 👍 
- Currently, I'm building a prototype of a constrained optimizer. Ideally, I hope that we can test how the optimizer I'm building will interface in the objective functions you are building 😄

## Getting Started
- To help you get started, please check the [Contributing guidelines](https://pyswarms.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html) in the documentation.
- When creating a git commit, please make sure that it follows this [prescribed format](http://tbaggery.com/2008/04/19/a-note-about-git-commit-messages.html):

```
Capitalized, short (50 chars or less) summary

More detailed explanatory text, if necessary.  Wrap it to about 72
characters or so.  In some contexts, the first line is treated as the
subject of an email and the rest of the text as the body.  The blank
line separating the summary from the body is critical (unless you omit
the body entirely); tools like rebase can get confused if you run the
two together.

Write your commit message in the imperative: "Fix bug" and not "Fixed bug"
or "Fixes bug."  This convention matches up with commit messages generated
by commands like git merge and git revert.

Further paragraphs come after blank lines.

- Bullet points are okay, too

- Typically a hyphen or asterisk is used for the bullet, followed by a
  single space, with blank lines in between, but conventions vary here

- Use a hanging indent

Author: your-github-username
E-mail: your-email
```

issue_title: Logic16 CommandNAKedError on save_to_file while in loop
issue_body:
I am attempting to write a test utility which will capture and save a new waveform every 20 seconds. After the first 2-3 successful iterations in the loop, the SaveDataToFile routine is raising CommandNAKedError which to my understanding of the docs means that the capture isn't complete when called or there is an issue with the filepath. As the first few files saved successfully, I doubt that the filepath is the issue, though watching the GUI the capture is definitely finished when I call save_to_file

I have attempted to wrap your library in a class of my own to allow for easy reuse. 

I was having trouble with the capture_start_and_wait_until_finished function, so I took the code in it and built my own which catches the NAK exception and ignores it. Is this the correct method for dealing with the NAK when calling this function before capture is complete? 

I'm using Python 3.5 / Logic 1.2.5

Here is the loop I am currently attempting to run and my class

```
l.StartCapture()
l.WaitUntilCaptureComplete()
l.SaveDataToFile(filepath + "\Error_" + str(count) + ".logicdata")
time.sleep(20)

```

```
class LogicAnalyzer:

    s = 0

    # Initializes the Saleae API
    def __init__(self, host='localhost', port=10429):
        self.s = saleae.Saleae(host,port)

        print("Connected to Logic")

        return

    # Starts the capture sequence
    def StartCapture(self):
        print("Starting capture")
        self.s.capture_start()
        return

    def WaitUntilCaptureComplete(self):
        done = False
        while done == False:
            try:	
                while not self.s.is_processing_complete():
                    print("\t..waiting for capture to complete")
                    time.sleep(1)

                print("Capture complete")

                done = True

            except self.s.CommandNAKedError:
                # This exception is raised if processing of capture is not complete on the
                # side of the analyzer. This is expected for the first few calls, therefore we 
                # simply ignore it until is_processing_complete returns true
                pass

    # Will save last capture in given output location/file
    # Filename must be absolute path
    # Folder must exist for file to be written
    def SaveDataToFile(self, filename):

        self.s.save_to_file(filename)
        print("File Saved")
        return

```

issue_title: diff-quality doesn't work with eslint
issue_body:
I have a git repo with python and javascript files. The structure is:
```
- docker
- scripts
- my_project
--- static
------ .eslintrc.js
------ package.json
------ js
--- app1
--- app2
```
The structure is typical for django projects.
Eslint generates reports with absolute path by default, e.g.
`/home/godlark/PycharmProjects/mye_repo/my_project/static/js/index.js`
but `diff-quality` expects paths relative to the root directory of repo, e.g.
`my_projects/static/js/index.js`
I was able to create a custom formatter to generate reports with relative path, e.g.
`js/index.js`
But it still doesn't match. The solution is to run in `diff-cover` command `git diff --relative` instead `git diff`

issue_title: Filtering (Searching) Timestamps by String Representation
issue_body: Angular Version: 1.4.9 Smart Table Version: 2.1.7 The angular filter function used in the `pipe` function of `stTableController` does a direct value comparison for filtering results. This is ideal for text based results, but when dealing with timestamps, the filter function will not match "March" up against a timestamp for the March timeframe. The only viable solution that I have been able to produce, is manipulating the data set and inserting a string based representation of the timestamp, as another key on the object. For Example: ``` var obj = { created_at: 1456929654000, created_at_search: '03/02/2016' }; ``` Is there a better solution out there for this concept? It seems extraneous to have to manipulate all timestamps to have an additional property on the object; especially when dealing with large datasets. It's also not applicable to just send the timestamp as a string representation, as for sorting; the numeric representation accurately handles ascending and descending order and the string representation would not be as accurate. Also, it is notable that the server's interpretation of the timezone, may not reflect the client's timezone (if handling the pre-rendered text server side). It would be ideal to have the controller understand the built in angular filters, as their rendered text: ``` {{ ::obj.created_at | date : 'MM/dd/yyyy' }} ```

issue_title: stSort: switching sort between two columns using getter functions
issue_body:
1. set up a sortable table where two columns use getter functions (not explicit properties).
2. Click on first column with getter.
3. Click on second column with getter.

The sort direction indicator in the header will not be updated. The table will sort properly, but the column indicator will remain on the previously sorted column.

I believe this is caused by the fact that angular.equals ignores functions when detecting equality. Therefore, the tableState().sort effectively remains equivalent if though the predicate has changed from one function to another. This prevents the watch function from being called (which is where the column classes are updated).

[Plunkr](http://plnkr.co/edit/DTkHIuJH9DCmGvDcyj9b) : Click on First Name col, then on Last Name col. The table sorts properly, but the sort indicator isn't updated properly. Note that if the two columns are in opposite forward/reverse states, then the sort indicator will update properly, since the tableState().sort in-equivalency will be detected by angular.equals...

Thanks!

issue_title: Bar(marker=AnimatedMarker()) not advancing
issue_body:
#### Description

When implementing AnimatedMarker in a ProgressBar, the Bar won't advance to the right and instead rotate in-place next to [left](http://progressbar-2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/progressbar/widgets.html#Bar). The code below reproduces the error.

#### Versions

- Python version: `2.7.15 (default, May 15 2018, 15:37:31) [GCC 7.3.1 20180303 (Red Hat 7.3.1-5)]`
- Package version: `3.38.0`

#### Code

```
import time
from progressbar import Bar, Counter, ETA, Percentage, ProgressBar, AnimatedMarker

test_size = 30

# Bar() advances just fine
widgets = ['Loading: ', Percentage(), ' ', Bar(), ' ', Counter(), '/{} '.format(test_size), ETA()]
pbar = ProgressBar(widgets=widgets, maxval=test_size).start()

for i in range(test_size):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    pbar.update(i)

pbar.finish()

# Marker rotates in-place on the right of the first |
widgets = ['Loading: ', Percentage(), ' ', Bar(marker=AnimatedMarker()), ' ', Counter(), '/{} '.format(test_size), ETA()]
pbar = ProgressBar(widgets=widgets, maxval=test_size).start()

for i in range(test_size):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    pbar.update(i)

pbar.finish()
```

issue_title: Print command output before returning shell
issue_body:
When shell commands are being executed and return one or more lines of output, `click-shell` currently prints a (new) prompt before the output is printed. Example:
* Command: `test`
* Command output: `This line is returned.`
* Prompt: `prompt >`
* Output:
```
prompt > test
prompt > This line is returned.
<new line>
```
* Expected output:
```
prompt > test
This line is returned.
prompt > 
```

Have you seen this behaviour before? After some debugging, my impression is that the described behaviour is a side-effect of [this `get_input()` call](https://github.com/clarkperkins/click-shell/blob/master/click_shell/_cmd.py#L94).

issue_title: Fails to create Revisions/Versions in custom management command using automatic admin registration method as described in docs
issue_body:
Looking [here](https://django-reversion.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#registering-models-with-django-reversion) in the docs it says:

> **Warning:** If you’re using django-reversion in an management command, and are using the automatic `VersionAdmin` registration method, then you’ll need to import the relevant `admin.py` file at the top of your management command file.

I'm pretty sure this doesn't work currently. If you setup your `admin.py` like this:

```py
# app/admin.py

from django.contrib import admin

import reversion

from app.models import Model


@admin.register(Model)
class ModelAdmin(reversion.VersionAdmin):
    pass
```

And you setup your management command like this:

```py
# app/management/commands/my_command.py

from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand

from app import admin  # "...import the relevant admin.py..."
from app.models import Model


class Command(BaseCommand):
    args = 'None'
    help = ('My Command')

    def handle(self, *args, **kwargs):
        m = Model(foo=bar)
        m.save()

```

Then when you run `python manage.py my_command` the instance is saved, but no `Revision` is created.

issue_title: Spurious FileExistsError
issue_body:
On https://github.com/ionelmc/pytest-benchmark/blob/master/src/pytest_benchmark/storage/file.py#L15, the check for `exists` followed by the call to mkdir() exhibits a race condition, which I sometimes manage to provoke with [detox](https://github.com/tox-dev/detox). A better pattern would be to try the .mkdir() and catch the appropriate OSError.

This is hard to reproduce (yay race conditions) but I've seen it several times now, and it happens when benchmark creates the storage directory even if it won't put anything there, so it is hard to avoid.

issue_title: validate_incoming_request masks error when route not properly defined
issue_body:
Ran up against a wall while adding a new route. It turns out I had misconfigured the route, so the true error was HTTP 404 Not Found, but pyramid_swagger masked the error message.

``` python
    def validate_incoming_request(route_mapper, request, schema_map, resolver):
        """Validates an incoming request against our schemas.

        :param request: the request object to validate
        :type request: Pyramid request object passed into a view
        :param schema_map: our mapping from request data to schemas (see
            load_schema)
        :type schema_map: dict
        :param resolver: the request object to validate
        :type resolver: Pyramid request object passed into a view
        :returns: None
        """
        if schema_map.request_query_schema:
            # You'll notice we use Draft3 some places and Draft4 in others.
            # Unfortunately this is just Swagger's inconsistency showing. It
            # may be nice in the future to do the necessary munging to make
            # everything Draft4 compatible, although the Swagger UI will
            # probably never truly support Draft4.
            request_query_params = dict(
                (k, cast_request_param(schema_map.request_query_schema, k, v))
                for k, v
                in request.GET.items()
            )
            Draft3Validator(
                schema_map.request_query_schema,
                resolver=resolver,
                types=EXTENDED_TYPES,
            ).validate(request_query_params)

        if schema_map.request_path_schema:
            # We don't have access to this yet but let's go ahead and build the
            # matchdict so we can validate it.
            info = route_mapper(request)
            matchdict = dict(
                (k, cast_request_param(schema_map.request_path_schema, k, v))
                for k, v
>               in info.get('match', {}).items()
            )
E           AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'items'
```

The problem appears to be `route_mapper(request)` sets `None` if there really is no route (due to misconfiguration):

``` python
(Pdb) info
{'route': None, 'match': None}
```

issue_title: scipy.optimize.OptimizeResult is not always an array
issue_body:
Hello,

## Thank you

Many (many) thanks for putting together this wonderful package and the great tutorial.
I am constructing a small API around this package to make it easy to tune machine learning hyper parameters and this package will be invaluable.


## Summary

When invoking `BayesianOptimization.maximize(init_points=init_points, n_iter=0)`, I have noticed that `helpers.acq_max()` can fail. In particular, the `scipy.optimize.OptimizeResult` object can be a scalar but the code expects an array. As a result, `helpers. acq_max()` can fail on these lines of code:

```
        # Store it if better than previous minimum(maximum).
        if max_acq is None or -res.fun[0] >= max_acq:
            x_max = res.x
            max_acq = -res.fun[0]
```


## Symptom details

```python
bo  = BayesianOptimization(f=obj_fnc, pbounds=hp_range)
bo.maximize(init_points=init_points, n_iter=0)

(...)

/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/site-packages/bayes_opt/bayesian_optimization.py in maximize(self, init_points, n_iter, acq, kappa, xi, **gp_params)
    262                         gp=self.gp,
    263                         y_max=y_max,
--> 264                         bounds=self.bounds)
    265 
    266         # Print new header

/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/site-packages/bayes_opt/helpers.py in acq_max(ac, gp, y_max, bounds)
     53 
     54         # Store it if better than previous minimum(maximum).
---> 55         if max_acq is None or -res.fun[0] >= max_acq:
     56             x_max = res.x
     57             max_acq = -res.fun[0]

IndexError: too many indices for array
```



## Fix proposal
I was able to solve the issue by cloning this repo and looking into `helpers. acq_max()` and replacing `-res.fun[0]` by `-res.fun.min()` in `helpers. acq_max()`.

```python
        # Store it if better than previous minimum(maximum).
        if max_acq is None or -res.fun.min() >= max_acq:
            x_max = res.x
            max_acq = -res.fun.min()
```


Once these modifications were made and (I changed the imports in my code to use the modified bases_opt package), the initialization step was able to complete successfully:

```
Initialization
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Step |   Time |      Value |   acc_improvement_factor |   batch_size |   lr_decay_factor |   lr_init |    window | 
    1 | 01m20s |    0.61373 |                   1.0335 |     249.4912 |            0.9903 |    0.0634 |   17.1281 | 
    2 | 01m22s |    0.37705 |                   1.0155 |     180.6748 |            0.9961 |    0.0647 |   14.6763 | 
Bayesian Optimization
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Step |   Time |      Value |   acc_improvement_factor |   batch_size |   lr_decay_factor |   lr_init |    window | 
```




## Disclaimer

I am new to Bayesian Optimization, `scipy.optimize.minimize` and `cipy.optimize.OptimizeResult` so the fix suggestion might not be correct. Feel free to close this issue as 'user error' if you feel my use case should not occur under normal circumstances (which means I am doing something wrong).

Hit me up if you have any questions or need more details.

Thanks again!

issue_title: Output a screenshot outside of Jupyter notebook / Headless support
issue_body:
cc @choldgraf 
ipyvolume is nice for exploring in the notebook, but when doing batch jobs people may want to output screenshots of simulations in a cluster/batch setting, not in a notebook (or at least not in the browser). It would be a shame if just for that you'd have to use another library.
At JupyterCon I've looked at this with @choldgraf and saw this was feasible. The current solution requires you to start chrome headless, and using the `headless=True` argument for savefig it will save the current viz to html, let chrome open that, make a screenshot, and save it to disk.

I'm opening this issue to focus the discussion here.
@choldgraf: would be nice if you could test this and give feedback if it really works in a headless environment.

The documentation is in the [docstring of ipyvolume.headless](https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/master/ipyvolume/headless.py)
after the next release it will also be at readthedocs.

```python
from io import BytesIO, TextIOWrapper
from six import PY2
if PY2:
    from backports import csv
else:
    import csv

issue_title: pytz dependency ('NoneType' object has no attribute 'timezone')
issue_body:
On [this line](https://github.com/spookylukey/django-paypal/blob/master/paypal/standard/forms.py#L63) dependency on ``pytz`` was added. But ``setup.py`` have no ``pytz`` in dependencies. And now I'm getting this error:

```
  File ".../python2.7/site-packages/paypal/standard/ipn/views.py", line 62, in ipn
    if form.is_valid():
  File "...python2.7/site-packages/django/forms/forms.py", line 184, in is_valid
    return self.is_bound and not self.errors
  File ".../python2.7/site-packages/django/forms/forms.py", line 176, in errors
    self.full_clean()
  File ".../python2.7/site-packages/django/forms/forms.py", line 392, in full_clean
    self._clean_fields()
  File ".../python2.7/site-packages/django/forms/forms.py", line 407, in _clean_fields
    value = field.clean(value)
  File ".../python2.7/site-packages/django/forms/fields.py", line 162, in clean
    value = self.to_python(value)
  File ".../python2.7/site-packages/paypal/standard/forms.py", line 63, in to_python
    dt = timezone.pytz.timezone('US/Pacific').localize(
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'timezone'
```

And for projects which were updated to the latest version of ``django-paypal`` and don't have installed ``pytz`` package this error is very critical. PayPal IPN stop working.

Simple fix is to check existence of ``pytz`` or add it to dependency. But I think this part of code could be revised.

issue_title: Pluralize and multiple language forms
issue_body:
Hi.
I may have misunderstood something, but I can't make it working.
In russian language nouns are pluralized into 4 forms, not 2. Example:
```
{% blocktrans count counter=stats['users_count'] %}Уникальный посетитель{% plural %}Уникальные посетители{% endblocktrans %}
```

And .po file:
```
#: apps/jinja2/emails/app_stats_email.jinja2:348
msgid "Уникальный посетитель"
msgid_plural "Уникальные посетители"
msgstr[0] "Уникальный посетитель"
msgstr[1] "Уникальных посетителя"
msgstr[2] "Уникальных посетителей"
```
If I understood correctly, django's template pluralization should output one of msgstr[0] - msgstr[2] depending on the counter. But it always outputs msgid_plural.

issue_title: [Feature Request] Split large messages onto smaller before sending
issue_body:
Some bots are intended to send large piles of data (news articles, for example). When sending large texts, you may encounter [HTTP Error 414](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes) ("Request URI Too Long")

In this case, I've started using the following function (found on [StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/a/9475354/2032848)):
```python
def string_splitter(line, chars_in_each_line):
    return [line[i:i + chars_in_each_line] for i in range(0, len(line), chars_in_each_line)]
```
It returns an array of chunks, `chars_in_each_line` long each. 
I can't say which number is max supported, but `2000` seems fine.
So, maybe you could add a parameter to send_message? (f.ex. `single_message_length`)

issue_title: flask-login, alternative-tokens is not implemented
issue_body:
Currently the login manager's user_loader is set to get a user by id.
This id is set using the get_id method of the User class.

There should be a simple way to replace the user_loader so that you can implement your own id token and invalidate user session when for example a users password is changed.

In the current implementation if a user changes their password, this does not invalidate other logged in sessions because they are all based on a static property, user.id

https://flask-login.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#alternative-tokens
